voiceover: You're anxious about everything AND you can't get anything done. So which one is it — ADHD or anxiety? Here's the thing: they look almost identical from the outside. Both cause avoidance. Both cause racing thoughts. Both make you stare at a task and freeze. But the reason is different. Anxiety avoids because it fears something bad will happen. ADHD avoids because the brain genuinely can't generate the activation to start. One is dread. One is a dead engine. If your avoidance comes with specific 'what ifs', lean anxiety. If it's more like blankness — no fear, just nothing — that's more ADHD. And a lot of people have both. So stop blaming your character. Your brain is working against a biology, not a personality flaw. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: ADHD or Anxiety? How to Tell the Difference #Shorts
description: ADHD and anxiety look almost identical — avoidance, racing thoughts, freezing up. But the reason behind the behavior is completely different, and knowing which one is driving you changes everything. This video breaks down the key difference in a simple, science-backed way common in ADHD and anxiety research. Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: Your brain won't start? Stop forcing it. Bribe it. A dopamine menu is a short list of tiny rewards you actually look forward to. You pick one before a hard task. Not after. Before. So your brain has something to chase. Step one: write down five to ten things that genuinely feel good. Coffee, a playlist, a snack, ten minutes outside. Step two: before a task you keep avoiding, pick one from the list. Step three: start the task with that reward already happening. Music on, coffee in hand. You are not cheating. You are finally speaking your brain's language. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Use a Dopamine Menu to Finally Start Hard Tasks #Shorts
description: If your ADHD brain refuses to start tasks, a dopamine menu can help. This simple technique uses small rewards before a task to give your brain the dopamine boost it needs to get going. It is not a hack, it is neuroscience. Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: You were called 'so mature for your age' — but really, you were just exhausted from holding yourself together. Late-diagnosed ADHD often looks like a kid who tried twice as hard just to seem normal. You memorized social scripts. You hid your messy notes. You apologized constantly. Not because you were bad — but because your brain needed tools nobody gave you. The masking worked well enough to fool everyone, including you. And now as an adult, you're finally getting answers. That 'laziness' was never laziness. Sound familiar? Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Signs You Grew Up Masking ADHD Without Knowing #Shorts
description: If you were always called 'mature' or 'too hard on yourself,' you might have been masking undiagnosed ADHD your whole life. Late-diagnosed ADHD is incredibly common, especially in people who learned to hide their struggles early. This is what it actually looks like — and why it was never a character flaw. Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: You become a different person depending on who you're with. That's Rejection Masking — changing yourself so no one has a reason to leave. It's exhausting, and it's incredibly common in ADHD. Here's what it looks like: You laugh at jokes you hate because disagreeing feels too risky. You shrink your opinions down to nothing in group chats. You say yes to plans you're dreading just to keep the peace. And alone, you can't even remember who you actually are anymore. If this is you, you're not fake — you're just wired to feel rejection really, really hard. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Rejection Masking: The ADHD Pattern You Hide #Shorts
description: Rejection Masking is a common ADHD pattern where you change who you are to avoid being disliked or rejected. It's linked to Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria and can make you feel like you've lost yourself. If this sounds like you, know that it's not a character flaw — it's how your ADHD brain is wired to protect you.

question: Which inbox makes you sweat most?

options.label: The avalanche
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A cozy illustrated email inbox icon overflowing with hundreds of stacked envelope shapes spilling out like an avalanche, envelopes tumbling in all directions, chaotic but softly drawn.
options.result: Your brain treats every unread message as equal-priority chaos - classic ADHD overwhelm flooding.

options.label: The 1 email
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A single solitary envelope sitting alone in a wide open empty inbox tray, spotlight-like glow around it, everything else perfectly bare and tidy around that one envelope.
options.result: One unresolved thing loops in your brain on repeat - ADHD working memory holding it hostage.

options.label: The read pile
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A neat stack of open envelopes all marked as read, arranged in a tidy pile, but a small separate corner pile of envelopes is slightly crumpled and ignored, two distinct zones visible.
options.result: You start strong then stall - ADHD task-initiation wins the open, loses the follow-through.

options.label: All of them
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. Four small inbox trays arranged in a cozy cluster, each one showing a different level of fullness - one empty, one overflowing, one with a single envelope, one half-sorted - all sitting together warmly.
options.result: Context-switching between inboxes drains you equally - your ADHD brain pays a tax every single time.

introVoiceover: Four inboxes. Pick the one that makes your stomach drop the most. Your choice says something real about how your ADHD brain handles pressure - drop it in the comments.
outroVoiceover: No wrong answer here - your brain is just wired differently. Follow for daily ADHD content.
title: Which inbox stresses you out most? (ADHD test) #Shorts
description:
Pick the inbox that makes you sweat - then watch as we reveal exactly what your choice says about your ADHD brain, one option at a time. The answers are all in the video.
Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: You felt something five minutes ago and you're still not over it. That's not being dramatic. That's ADHD. The ADHD brain has a weak emotional brake. The prefrontal cortex, the part that says 'calm down, it's fine,' is under-connected. So when a feeling fires, it fires full volume. Rejection, frustration, embarrassment, even excitement, they all arrive like emergencies. Scientists call this emotional dysregulation, and it's one of the most exhausting parts of ADHD that almost nobody talks about. Here's what helps. First, name it fast. Saying 'this is dysregulation, not reality' interrupts the spiral before it locks in. Second, change your body state before your thoughts. Cold water on your wrists, a few slow exhales, even standing up. Your nervous system responds to physical input faster than logic. Third, give yourself a ten-minute window before you react to anything that stings. The feeling is real. The emergency usually isn't. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Why ADHD Feelings Hit So Much Harder #Shorts
description:
ADHD emotional dysregulation means your brain's emotional brake is weaker than most, making every feeling arrive at full volume. This isn't being dramatic, it's neuroscience, and there are real strategies that help. Learn why ADHD emotions feel so intense and what you can actually do about it.
Follow for daily ADHD content.
